title: 90 Days to First Paying AI Automation Client

weeks.title: Positioning and Setup
weeks.summary: Before any tools, you lock who you sell to, what you sell, for how much, and where you show up. Seven days of foundation work that every subsequent week depends on.

days.title: Define your ICP in one sentence
days.weekMission: Have a positioned offer, ready to sell, by end of Week 0
days.hook: You are going to spend the next 89 days building, selling, and delivering. Every single day. If your ICP is wrong, all 89 of those days produce waste. Two hours today saves you two months later. This is the highest-leverage single hour of the entire program.
days.objective: Write a single sentence that names exactly who you sell to. If it takes two sentences, you do not have an ICP yet.
learningOutcome: State in one sentence who you serve and what problem you solve
Verify your ICP passes the Specific, Verifiable, Painful test
List 20 real companies that match it by name

preWork.instructions: Before watching any videos, take 10 minutes and write, messily, no editing, answers to these questions in a scratch doc. Do not research. Write your gut answer. You will compare to Nick Saraev's frameworks after.
prompts: Who am I pissed on behalf of? Whose problem makes me think someone should fix this?
What industry or company type do I understand better than most people because of SmartClick plus LEORON plus my outreach reps?
If I had to pick only one type of company to sell to for the rest of 2026, who would it be?

videos.title: How to Pick a Niche for your AI Automation Agency in 2025
videos.creator: Nick Saraev
keyTimestamps: Watch all of it at 1.25x speed
Focus on the 3 qualifiers he names for a good niche

videos.title: The 3 Best Niches For AI Automation Agencies
videos.creator: Nick Saraev
keyTimestamps: First 8 minutes only - the what makes a good niche framework
Stop when he starts listing the 3 niches, you already know SaaS is one

steps: Write your ICP using this template, filling each bracket: I help [SIZE] [INDUSTRY] companies [SPECIFIC PAINFUL PROBLEM] by [SPECIFIC AUTOMATION SOLUTION].

Run the 3-part test and write YES or NO next to each: Specific - replace your ICP with 'businesses', still make sense? If yes, too vague. Verifiable - can I find 100 of them on LinkedIn in 5 minutes? Painful - would they pay $2K+ to solve the problem I named?
Compress the sentence to 10 words maximum. If you cannot, refine.
Save as ICP.md in your ammar-blueprint folder.

tasks: Sentence passes Specific, Verifiable, Painful test
stretchGoals: List 20 companies that match your ICP exactly using LinkedIn and Apollo free tier
For 5 of those, find the founder or head of GTM LinkedIn profile. Save as target-accounts-v1.md
completionCriteria: ICP.md file exists in project folder
Three-part test passed
Sentence is 10 words or fewer

publicCommitment.template: Day 1 of my 90-day AI automation build. Here is who I am building for: [your ICP sentence]. Documenting everything at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app

days.reflectionPrompt: If someone asked me at a dinner party who do you help, could I answer in one breath, without notes, and have them say oh I know someone like that? If not, what word is missing from my ICP?

days.title: Design your offer and lock the price
days.weekMission: Have a positioned offer, ready to sell, by end of Week 0
days.hook: Most AI automation freelancers fail here. They have vague offers like I build AI automations and they quote hourly. You are going to name one specific thing you sell, at one specific price, for one specific outcome. Clarity wins deals. Vagueness loses them. Nick Saraev and his community do not pitch I can build automations. They pitch specific systems: $4K email infrastructure setup. $3,800/month retainer for automated lead qualification. $8K retainer for full outbound engine. Named offer. Named price.
days.objective: Turn who you sell to into what you sell, at what price, for what outcome. One productized offer with three tiers.
learningOutcome: Name your productized offer in one sentence with a price tag attached
Defend the price based on value delivered, not hours worked
Articulate the 30-90 day outcome the buyer gets

preWork.instructions: Answer in your scratch doc, no research.
prompts: Looking at your ICP, what is the most expensive human problem they have that is within your skill reach?
If you had to guess their monthly waste from that problem in dollars, what is the number?
If your offer would save them 10x what it costs, what is the ceiling on your price?

videos.title: How I Onboard AI Automation Agency Retainer Clients
videos.creator: Nick Saraev
keyTimestamps: Focus: how he positions retainers, not just one-off builds
Watch at 1.25x

videos.title: How I Hit $25K/mo Selling Automation Services (Roadmap and Guide)
videos.creator: Nick Saraev
keyTimestamps: 30:00-55:00 for pricing anchors
Skip the intro and niche sections

steps: Create OFFER.md in your project folder.
Write section 'What I sell': one sentence, named system plus target outcome.
Write section 'Who it is for': copy your ICP from Day 1, no edits.
Write section 'The outcome': what the buyer has in 30 days that they did not have before.
Write section 'Price anchor' with three tiers: Tier 1 Audit $500-750 one-time, Tier 2 Setup Sprint $2,500-3,500 one-time, Tier 3 Retainer $1,500-2,500 per month with 3-month minimum.
Write section 'Why this price' with 3-5 bullets using value language, not hours language.
Write section 'Who this is NOT for' with at least 3 exclusions.

tasks: Three price tiers defined with actual dollar amounts
Why this price uses value language not hours language
Who this is NOT for has at least 3 real exclusions
stretchGoals: Calculate the ROI math: if their problem costs $X per month, your fix pays back in N months. Turn it into a simple sentence you could say on a cold call.
Draft a 3-bullet FAQ: What if it breaks? What if we do not have the integrations? Can I cancel the retainer?
completionCriteria: OFFER.md exists with all required sections
Can read OFFER.md aloud in under 90 seconds
Would make a SaaS founder say tell me more

publicCommitment.template: Day 2 of my 90-day AI automation build. Offer locked. [one sentence describing what you sell]. Pricing starts at $[X]. Send me a DM if you are a B2B SaaS founder bleeding on [specific pain].

days.reflectionPrompt: If a founder said that is too expensive, would I defend the price or discount it? If I would discount, my price is not anchored in real value yet. What proof do I need to stop flinching at price objections?

days.title: Upwork profile overhaul
days.weekMission: Have a positioned offer, ready to sell, by end of Week 0
days.hook: In 2026, Upwork's algorithm rewards specificity. Niche profiles get 75% more invitations than generalist ones. The freelancers at $100 per hour have 2 things in common: one clearly-named specialty plus 3-5 embedded keywords that match what clients search. You are not competing with 2 million freelancers. You are competing with the handful who have optimized their profile for your exact niche. Today you become one of them.
days.objective: Ship a live Upwork profile that someone looking for YOUR offer would hire on first view.
learningOutcome: Describe your Upwork profile angle in one sentence without hedging
Point to the exact 5 keywords clients will use to find you
Ship a live profile that reflects your offer and positioning

preWork.instructions: Go to Upwork. Search for 3 job postings that match your ICP. Reverse-engineer what wins before writing your profile.
prompts: Search 'AI automation' plus 'SaaS' - copy the exact job title and 5 most-used phrases
Search 'n8n' plus 'B2B' - same
Search 'lead generation automation' - same, then click the top 3 bidders' profiles and note their angles

videos.title: Upwork Account Guide: Expert Steps for Success in 2026
videos.creator: Getmany (read article)
keyTimestamps: Focus on profile SEO and keyword density sections

videos.title: Upwork profile optimization for AI automation freelancers
videos.creator: Search YouTube 2026 videos
keyTimestamps: Pick the newest video with 50K+ views

steps: Rebuild your Upwork profile title (headline, 70 char max): outcome verb plus ICP plus specific mechanism. Example: I build AI lead-routing systems for B2B SaaS teams bleeding on response time.
Set hourly rate to $50-75 minimum. Low rates filter for low-quality buyers.

Rewrite Overview (1,500-2,000 chars): outcome opening line, who you help, what you build, proof including GitHub and ammar-blueprint.vercel.app plus SmartClick B2B SaaS experience, who it is NOT for, CTA offering a free 2-minute Loom audit.

Update Skills to 10-15 niche-specific: n8n, Make.com, AI Workflow Automation, Lead Scoring, Apollo, Instantly, Cold Email Infrastructure, CRM Integration, OpenAI API, Claude API, SaaS Sales Operations.
Add Portfolio: ammar-blueprint.vercel.app and GitHub profile link.

stretchGoals: Record and upload a 60-90 second Loom video intro
Add a Specialized Profile for AI Automation
Take Upwork free skill tests in n8n, automation, or API integration to earn badges
completionCriteria: Profile is live and public on Upwork
Could send your profile link to a SaaS founder and feel proud, not apologetic

publicCommitment.template: Day 3 of 90. Upwork profile live as an AI automation specialist for B2B SaaS. If you know a SaaS founder with a broken lead flow, introduce us.

days.reflectionPrompt: If a buyer landed on my profile right now and had to decide in 10 seconds whether I am their guy, what single sentence would convince them? Is that sentence in my headline?

days.title: LinkedIn alignment and first content piece
days.weekMission: Have a positioned offer, ready to sell, by end of Week 0
days.hook: When a Western B2B SaaS founder sees your Upwork bid, their next move is to Google you. They will find your LinkedIn. If it says SDR at SmartClick with no mention of AI automation, they will assume you are learning on their dime. Today you align LinkedIn so it backs up your Upwork positioning WITHOUT creating conflict with your actual SmartClick role. SmartClick sells SEO/AEO/GEO. Your side offer builds AI automation. Different lanes. No conflict.
days.objective: Ship an aligned LinkedIn profile plus one public post that demonstrates your skill.
learningOutcome: Show up on LinkedIn as B2B SaaS outbound specialist who builds AI automation
Write 1 post that demonstrates your skill publicly
Name the 3 content pillars you will repeat every week

preWork.instructions: Screenshot your current LinkedIn profile. You will compare before and after tonight.
prompts: What is my current headline?
What is the first thing a visitor sees above the fold?
If a founder clicked my profile tomorrow, would they get why I am worth a call?

videos.title: Justin Welsh LinkedIn Operating System
videos.creator: Justin Welsh
keyTimestamps: Skim his posts tagged LinkedIn
Focus on headline formula, featured section, and 3-pillar content strategy

steps: Update headline formula: [what I do] | [who I help] | [proof or unique angle]. Example: Building AI automation systems for B2B SaaS | B2B outbound specialist | Public builder at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app
Rewrite About section: hook, who you help, what you build, proof and background, current bet (the 90-day build), CTA.
Pin 3 items to Featured section: ammar-blueprint.vercel.app, GitHub profile, and your first post from step 4.
Write and publish your first content post using the hook formula: Line 1 specific claim or contrarian view, Line 2-3 why it matters, Line 4-7 framework or insight, Line 8 what you are doing about it.
Save CONTENT_PILLARS.md with 3 pillars: Build in public (3 posts/week), Teach the niche (2 posts/week), Opinion or contrarian takes (1 post/week).

stretchGoals: Connect to 10 new B2B SaaS founders via Sales Navigator free trial or LinkedIn search
Turn on Creator Mode
Draft the next 3 posts in LinkedIn drafts, scheduled for the week
completionCriteria: LinkedIn profile passes the could this person plausibly charge $2-5K for AI automation work sniff test
First post is live and public

publicCommitment.template: Already fulfilled by publishing your first post today.

days.reflectionPrompt: Reading my old headline and my new headline back-to-back, which version would I hire? Why?
days.artifact: Live LinkedIn profile + first published post + CONTENT_PILLARS.md

days.title: GitHub portfolio and public README
days.weekMission: Have a positioned offer, ready to sell, by end of Week 0
days.hook: A Western B2B SaaS founder evaluating you will check: Upwork (done Day 3), LinkedIn (done Day 4), GitHub (today). If GitHub shows an empty profile or a single repo with no README, you look like a hobbyist. If it shows 2-3 public repos with clean READMEs, you look like a builder. Your ammar-blueprint repo is live. Today you turn it into a proper public portfolio piece.
days.objective: Transform your GitHub from a scratchpad into a portfolio link you can paste into any proposal.
learningOutcome: Describe what ammar-blueprint is in a public README a stranger can understand
Have a GitHub profile page that signals professional builder
Own a portfolio link you can paste into any proposal

preWork.instructions: Visit 3 top n8n or automation freelancers' GitHub profiles. Find them via Upwork, Twitter, or n8n creators list. Pattern-match the structure.
prompts: What do their pinned repos look like?
Do they have a profile README? What does it say?
Is there a bio line and contact info?

videos.title: Managing your GitHub profile README (official docs)
videos.creator: GitHub Docs
keyTimestamps: Read the whole thing, it is short

videos.title: Best GitHub profile README examples 2026
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Pick any 10-min video, get visual ideas

steps: Improve ammar-blueprint/README.md: what this is, live site URL, stack, 90-day goal, progress checklist, why public, contact info. Commit and push.
Create a new public repo on GitHub with your username as both the owner and repo name. Initialize with README.
Write profile README: your name plus one-sentence positioning, location, languages, current role, what you do outside SmartClick, current 90-day public build with link, stack, contact links.
Pin ammar-blueprint to your GitHub profile via 'Customize your pins'.
Test the URL in incognito mode. See what a stranger sees.

stretchGoals: Add 2 more small public repos: a simple n8n workflow template as JSON, or a cold email template you have built
Add GitHub stats cards to your profile README (search 'github readme stats' for free widgets)
completionCriteria: A stranger landing on your GitHub profile for the first time can say in 5 seconds 'this person builds AI automation'

publicCommitment.template: Day 5 of 90. My GitHub profile is now a portfolio, not a graveyard. [your URL]

days.reflectionPrompt: If an engineer at a B2B SaaS company saw my GitHub, would they think I am serious or hobbying? What one thing would double the credibility signal?
days.artifact: Public GitHub profile + improved ammar-blueprint README

days.title: First 10 target accounts and 3 DMs drafted
days.weekMission: Have a positioned offer, ready to sell, by end of Week 0
days.hook: You are a trained SDR. You know a cold pipeline takes 2-3 weeks to warm up. By drafting your first 10 target accounts today, you give Claude Code, your portfolio, and your LinkedIn posts time to mature BEFORE you contact these prospects. When you do reach out in Week 5, your profile will show 5 weeks of public building, making you look established, not desperate.
days.objective: Research 10 ICP-matched target accounts and draft 3 personalized DMs, held for Week 5 outreach.
learningOutcome: Find 10 ICP-matched B2B SaaS companies with named decision-makers
Draft 3 personalized DMs tailored to each prospect's specific pain
Know the difference between relevant and warm prospects

preWork.instructions: Answer in scratch doc.
prompts: Which ICP signals are visible on LinkedIn? Examples: hiring SDR, fundraising announcement, head of sales left.
What would make me KNOW a prospect has the problem my offer solves?
Which of my languages might give me an unfair edge on any of these?

videos.title: Apollo free guide to B2B prospecting with signals
videos.creator: Apollo
keyTimestamps: Focus on signal-based prospecting section

videos.title: How to find clients on LinkedIn for AI agencies
videos.creator: Nick Saraev or similar
keyTimestamps: Pick the highest-viewed relevant video

steps: Open a Google Sheet called TARGETS_V1. Columns: Rank, Company, ARR est, Founder/Head of GTM, LinkedIn URL, Signal, Specific Pain, Language edge.

Fill 10 rows from sources: LinkedIn search for Head of Sales plus SaaS plus US/UK/Netherlands, Apollo free tier filtered by industry/size/geography, X/Twitter follows of B2B SaaS founders, Crunchbase recent Series A/B SaaS.
Rank 1-10 by fit. Rank 1 is perfect match. Rank 10 is plausible match.

Draft 3 DMs in DRAFT_DMS.md for ranks 1, 2, 3. Structure: Line 1 specific observation about them (their post, hire, product, NOT generic flattery), Line 2 question tied to pain you solve, Line 3 soft offer (free Loom audit, not paid pitch).
DO NOT send today. They go in the outbox for Week 5.

tasks: 10 companies in TARGETS_V1 sheet with all columns filled
Each row has: named founder, LinkedIn URL, specific signal, pain hypothesis
stretchGoals: Expand to 25 targets
Add a column: Free Loom audit idea with one specific system you would build for each
Screenshot the 3 companies' tech stacks from BuiltWith or Wappalyzer
completionCriteria: If a friend read your DM drafts blind, they could NOT tell you copy-pasted a template

publicCommitment.template: Day 6 of 90. 10 target accounts researched. 3 personal DMs drafted. Sending in Week 5. Until then, I am building.

days.reflectionPrompt: Out of my 10 targets, which ONE would I sacrifice pricing to land? That is my whale. What do I know about them that nobody else pitching them knows?

days.title: Week 0 review plus human sanity check
days.weekMission: Have a positioned offer, ready to sell, by end of Week 0
days.hook: You have spent 6 days writing an ICP, offer, profile, content, and target list. It all sounds great to YOU because you wrote it. But you are not your buyer. Today you hand the work to someone and watch them react, before you invest Week 1 into building technical skills around the wrong positioning.
days.objective: Ship your Week 0 artifacts to one human reviewer, receive feedback, and revise the weakest item before Week 1.
learningOutcome: Receive critical feedback without defending
Identify which Week 0 output needs revision before Week 1
Commit to Week 1 with real conviction, not just hope

preWork.instructions: Pick your reviewer, ranked by signal quality.
prompts: Option 1: Milan or Boris at SmartClick - best signal since they sell to similar ICPs
Option 2: A former LEORON colleague - knows B2B sales, knows you
Option 3: A trusted friend who runs a business - outside view, still commercial
Option 4: Reddit r/Entrepreneur post - least personal, most anonymous

videos.title: How to get feedback on your product
videos.creator: Rob Walling or Justin Jackson
keyTimestamps: Key concepts: people lie politely, 'would you buy this' is worthless, 'what would stop you from buying this' is gold

steps: Create WEEK_0_REVIEW_PACK.md in your project. Include: ICP sentence, Offer summary condensed to half a page, LinkedIn headline + About, Upwork profile URL, 3 drafted DMs.
Add at the top: I am not asking you to cheer me on. I am asking: if a SaaS founder landed on each of these, what is the ONE thing that would stop them from replying or booking a call?

Call or message your reviewer. Share the pack. Ask these questions in order: Read the offer, in your own words what am I selling? Look at the Upwork profile, on a scale of 1-10 how serious do I look? Read the 3 DMs, would you reply to any of them and why? What is missing?
Write feedback verbatim in WEEK_0_FEEDBACK.md.
Revise the 1-2 weakest items based on feedback. Do NOT rewrite everything. Pick the highest-leverage fix and ship today.

tasks: One human has reviewed the Week 0 pack
At least one Week 0 artifact has been revised based on feedback
stretchGoals: Get feedback from 2 reviewers, not 1
Record a 15-min Loom walkthrough of your Week 0 work and share on X or LinkedIn
Write a Week 0 retrospective post: what I built, what surprised me, what is next
completionCriteria: You can answer YES to: if my target prospect saw my Upwork/LinkedIn/GitHub today, they would take me seriously as an AI automation consultant

publicCommitment.template: Week 0 complete. ICP, offer, profiles, portfolio, target list - all shipped. Week 1 starts Monday: learning n8n. Track it at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app.

days.reflectionPrompt: Looking back at 7 days of work, what surprised me? What did I resist writing because it felt too specific or too exposed? That resistance is usually the signal - the specific scary thing is the RIGHT thing.

weeks.title: n8n Foundations
weeks.summary: Learn what n8n is, install it, and build your first 3 real workflows. By end of week, you have a working automation that solves a real problem, not a toy tutorial. n8n is your primary automation platform for the rest of the program.
weeks.theme: From zero to first working automation

days.title: What n8n is and why it wins in 2026
days.weekMission: Ship your first 3 real n8n workflows
days.hook: You spent Week 0 on positioning. That work is worthless without a tool that delivers the outcome you promised. n8n is the tool. Today you understand why n8n (not Make, not Zapier) is the right choice for building AI automation for B2B SaaS in 2026. You will not touch a single workflow today. Understanding comes before doing.
days.objective: Understand what n8n is, how it is architected, and why it is the right primary tool for your positioning.
learningOutcome: Explain what n8n is in one sentence to a non-technical founder
Name the 3 reasons n8n wins over Make or Zapier for AI automation work
Decide between self-hosted and n8n cloud based on your situation

preWork.instructions: Before any videos, write your current understanding in a scratch doc. No research.
prompts: What do I think n8n does? (one sentence, guess)
If I had to pick Zapier, Make, or n8n for my offer, which would I pick and why?
What is the ONE question about n8n I want answered today?

videos.title: N8n Tutorial for Beginners 2026: Part 1 - Build Your First AI Workflow
videos.creator: n8n Official Community
keyTimestamps: Watch the overview sections only today, not the build
Focus on what n8n is and how nodes work

videos.title: Make.com vs n8n in 2025 (and 2026)
videos.creator: Nick Saraev
keyTimestamps: His honest head-to-head, biased slightly toward n8n for AI work
Match his reasoning against your own offer

steps: Write N8N_DECISION.md in your project folder.
Section 1: one-sentence definition of n8n in your own words.
Section 2: 3 bullets on why n8n wins for AI automation work (cite sources).

Section 3: self-hosted vs cloud decision, with your pick and reasoning. For Week 1, recommend starting with n8n cloud free trial - zero setup friction, you switch to self-hosted later if retainers justify the infrastructure time.
Section 4: the ONE concern you still have about n8n.

tasks: Picked cloud vs self-hosted with justification
stretchGoals: Sign up for n8n cloud free trial (do not build yet, just get the account)
Read the n8n docs homepage (10 min)
Join the n8n Discord or community forum
completionCriteria: Can explain n8n in one sentence without notes
Know whether you are using cloud or self-hosted for Week 1

publicCommitment.template: Day 8 of 90. Picked n8n as my automation platform for AI work. Reason: [your reason in 10 words]. Why not [alternative]? [one sentence]. Building in public at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app

days.reflectionPrompt: If I had to pitch n8n to a SaaS founder in 60 seconds, what is the single strongest reason? Is that reason something my ICP would actually value, or something I as a builder value?

days.title: Install n8n and meet the interface
days.weekMission: Ship your first 3 real n8n workflows
days.hook: You understand n8n conceptually. Today you meet it hands-on. No building yet, just orientation. Think of it like learning a kitchen before you cook. Where are the knives, where is the stove, where is the trash. Tomorrow you cook.
days.objective: Have n8n running and be able to navigate its interface comfortably.
learningOutcome: Access n8n from your browser
Identify the 5 core interface elements: canvas, node panel, execution log, workflow list, settings
Understand what nodes are and how they connect

preWork.instructions: Clear your desk, close other browser tabs, pick one 90-minute block. This is hands-on work.
prompts: What time am I doing this? (put it in your calendar)
What is my n8n cloud account email? (have it ready)

videos.title: How to Set Up Your First n8n Automation: A Complete Beginner's Guide 2026
videos.creator: AI Tool Clinic
keyTimestamps: Focus on setup + interface sections
Skip the Gmail-to-Slack build for today, just orientation

steps: Sign up for n8n cloud free trial at n8n.io (if you did this as stretch yesterday, skip).
Log in. Take a screenshot of the empty dashboard.
Click 'Add workflow'. You are now on the canvas. Do not build, just look.
Click 'Add first step'. The node picker opens. Scroll through node categories: Triggers, Actions, AI, Data Transformation, Flow.
Close the node picker without adding anything. Click any empty canvas area.
Open the left sidebar. Find: Workflows, Credentials, Executions, Templates.
Open a public template from the Templates page (any template). Read through its nodes. Do not activate.
Go back to your empty workflow.
Save INTERFACE_NOTES.md in your project folder with: the 5 most important UI elements you noticed and what each does.

tasks: Navigated the canvas, node picker, and sidebar
stretchGoals: Browse 10 templates in the Templates section, note 3 you want to adapt later
Read n8n docs: 'Understand n8n' section (10 min)
completionCriteria: Can find: canvas, node picker, workflow list, execution log, credentials section
Feel comfortable clicking around without fear of breaking anything

publicCommitment.template: Day 9 of 90. n8n installed and running. Tomorrow I build my first workflow. Documenting the journey from SDR to AI automation builder at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app

days.reflectionPrompt: What surprised me about n8n? What feels more intuitive than I expected, and what feels more complex? The honest answer here tells me where to spend time next.

days.title: Your first workflow: triggers and actions
days.weekMission: Ship your first 3 real n8n workflows
days.hook: Today you build. Not a toy tutorial where you follow someone else's clicks and learn nothing. A simple, real workflow that does one thing well: triggers on a schedule and sends you a Slack or email message. If your future client asks 'can you automate something that runs every Monday morning?' - the answer becomes yes after today.
days.objective: Build, test, and activate your first working n8n workflow with a trigger and one action.
learningOutcome: Add a trigger node and configure it
Add an action node and connect it to the trigger
Test workflow execution manually and schedule it to run automatically
Read the execution log to debug

preWork.instructions: Pick the action you want today's workflow to take.
prompts: Do I want the workflow to send to my email, Slack, or Telegram? Pick one.
What time should it fire each day? (for testing, pick 5 min from now)
What message do I want it to send? (one line)

videos.title: Your first workflow (n8n official docs tutorial)
videos.creator: n8n Official
keyTimestamps: Read it as you build, not before
Adapt the tutorial to your chosen action (email or Slack, not NASA API)

steps: Create a new workflow. Name it 'Daily Reminder - Test'.
Add Schedule Trigger node. Configure: fire once per day at a time 5 minutes from now.
Add Gmail, Slack, or Telegram action node (based on your pre-work pick). Configure credentials.
Connect trigger to action. Set the message body to a static string like: 'Daily reminder from n8n - it works.'
Click 'Execute workflow' manually. Verify you receive the message.
Activate the workflow (toggle at top right). Wait 5 minutes. Verify it fires automatically.
Deactivate the workflow (you do not want daily spam).
Open the Executions tab. See your runs listed. Click one to see the data flow.
Save FIRST_WORKFLOW.md: one paragraph describing what the workflow does, one sentence on what broke or surprised you.

tasks: Workflow 'Daily Reminder - Test' exists in n8n
stretchGoals: Add a second action: after sending the message, also log it to a Google Sheet
Try modifying the trigger to run every Monday at 9am instead of daily
completionCriteria: You built and ran your first workflow end to end
You understand the trigger + action model

publicCommitment.template: Day 10 of 90. First n8n workflow shipped. Triggers on schedule, sends me a message. Sounds trivial, but it is the foundation every AI automation builds on. Live build log at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app

days.reflectionPrompt: What took longer than expected? What was easier than expected? The gap between expectation and reality tells me where my mental model of n8n is wrong.

days.title: HTTP Requests: talk to any API
days.weekMission: Ship your first 3 real n8n workflows
days.hook: Most of what you will build involves APIs: pulling leads from Apollo, pushing data to HubSpot, getting data from OpenAI. n8n has native integrations for ~400 apps, but the HTTP Request node is the universal key. It talks to anything. Master it today and your automation ceiling disappears.
days.objective: Make authenticated API calls from n8n using the HTTP Request node.
learningOutcome: Use the HTTP Request node to call a public API
Add authentication headers to call a protected API
Parse JSON response and use specific fields downstream

preWork.instructions: Read without judgment: this is where n8n feels technical for the first time. That is normal. You are not expected to memorize HTTP methods today.
prompts: What is my mental model of what an API does? (sentence)
What is the difference between GET and POST? (guess, then check after)

videos.title: n8n HTTP Request node tutorial
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Pick a video from 2025 or 2026
Focus on: headers, query parameters, body, response parsing

steps: Create a new workflow: 'API Practice'.
Add Manual Trigger (not Schedule, you want to click to run).
Add HTTP Request node. Configure: GET request to https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users (a public test API, no auth needed).
Execute. See the array of users returned in the output panel.
Add a Set node after HTTP Request. Use it to extract just the name and email fields for each user.
Execute again. Verify you see clean data.
Now the harder one: sign up for free OpenWeatherMap API (or any free API requiring a key).
Add credentials in n8n: Settings > Credentials > New > Header Auth. Name it, set header name (often 'Authorization' or 'X-API-Key'), paste your key.
Add second HTTP Request node in a new workflow or the same one. Configure to use the credential you created. Make a GET request to the weather API for any city.
Execute. Verify authenticated response.
Save API_PRACTICE.md: describe what you built and what clicked for you about how APIs work.

tasks: Set node used to extract specific fields
Credentials created in n8n for authenticated API
stretchGoals: Chain 2 API calls: use data from the first call to construct the second
Try a POST request instead of GET
completionCriteria: You can call any public API from n8n
You know how to add an API key securely via credentials
You can read JSON responses and use fields downstream

publicCommitment.template: Day 11 of 90. n8n HTTP Request node unlocked. I can now pull data from any API and pipe it into workflows. Every AI automation agency build starts here.

days.reflectionPrompt: Before today, APIs felt abstract to me. After today, how do they feel? What changed in my head about what automation can do?

days.title: Error handling and when things break
days.weekMission: Ship your first 3 real n8n workflows
days.hook: Workflows fail. APIs time out, data comes back malformed, services go down. A workflow that handles errors gracefully is the difference between 'cool demo' and 'production system a client pays $2K/mo for'. Today you learn to build workflows that do not break your client's business when the internet has a bad day.
days.objective: Implement error handling in n8n workflows so failures are caught, logged, and alerted, not silently swallowed.
learningOutcome: Use the Error Trigger node to catch failures
Route errors to a notification channel (email, Slack)
Add retry logic to flaky API calls
Know when 'fail fast' is better than 'retry silently'

preWork.instructions: Think about failures before you learn to handle them.
prompts: What would happen to my Day 11 weather workflow if the API is down when it fires?
If a client's lead routing workflow failed silently for 3 days, what is the cost to them?
What signal do I want when a workflow breaks - quiet log, email, Slack ping, SMS?

videos.title: n8n error handling workflow tutorial
videos.creator: Search YouTube 2025-2026
keyTimestamps: Focus on Error Trigger node and error workflows
Skip generic 'what is error handling' intros

steps: Open Day 11's API Practice workflow.
In the HTTP Request node settings, find 'Retry on Fail'. Enable it. Set max retries to 3, wait 5 seconds between.
In the same node, under 'Continue on Fail', set it to 'Continue' (so workflow does not halt).
Create a separate 'Error Handler' workflow. Add Error Trigger node as first node.
After Error Trigger, add Gmail or Slack action that sends YOU a message: 'n8n workflow failed: {error details}'.
Go to your main workflow's Settings. Set its 'Error Workflow' to the Error Handler you just built.
Deliberately break your main workflow: change the API URL to something invalid.
Execute. Watch the error propagate to your Error Handler. Verify you got the notification.
Fix the URL back. Execute again. Verify normal run.
Save ERROR_HANDLING.md: describe the pattern and one scenario where you would use retry vs fail-fast.

tasks: Main workflow has retry logic on HTTP node
Error Handler workflow exists and is wired to main workflow
stretchGoals: Add a condition: only retry on specific error codes (e.g. 500, 503), fail fast on 401/403
Log all errors to a Google Sheet for a running audit trail
completionCriteria: Workflow fails gracefully, not silently
You get notified when something breaks
Production-grade workflow you could sell a client with a straight face

publicCommitment.template: Day 12 of 90. Error handling added. Most automation tutorials skip this. This is what separates a demo from a system a B2B SaaS team trusts.

days.reflectionPrompt: What scares me more: a workflow that fails loudly, or one that fails silently? Which does my ICP fear more?
days.artifact: Main workflow + Error Handler workflow + ERROR_HANDLING.md

days.title: Build a real automation: lead enrichment MVP
days.weekMission: Ship your first 3 real n8n workflows
days.hook: Today is the payoff for Days 8-12. You build a real lead enrichment workflow - the kind you will actually sell. Input: a company name. Output: that company enriched with domain, LinkedIn, industry, and size, ready to drop into Apollo or a Google Sheet. This is v0.1 of the SDR-in-a-Box system you will later sell for $2-5K.
days.objective: Build a working lead enrichment workflow end to end: input row from Google Sheet, enriched output back to Google Sheet.
learningOutcome: Combine Google Sheets trigger, HTTP Request, and Google Sheets action in one workflow
Use a real enrichment API (Clearbit, Apollo, or public sources)
Handle the data transformation between nodes

preWork.instructions: Set up the raw materials before building.
prompts: Create a Google Sheet with columns: Company Name, Domain, LinkedIn URL, Industry, Size, Enriched At.
Add 5 real company names in the first column (pick from your Day 6 target accounts).
Pick your enrichment source: Apollo API (you have free tier), Clearbit free tier, or scrape from public web via simple Google search API.

videos.title: n8n Google Sheets integration tutorial
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Focus on Google Sheets trigger and action nodes
Skip fluff intros

steps: New workflow: 'Lead Enrichment v0.1'.
Add Google Sheets Trigger (new row added). Connect to your sheet.
Add HTTP Request node: call your enrichment API with the Company Name from the triggered row.
Add Set node: map API response fields to your sheet columns (domain, linkedin, industry, size).
Add Google Sheets action: Update row with enriched fields, match on company name.
Add error handling (use yesterday's pattern).
Test: add a new row to your sheet, manually trigger workflow, verify enrichment lands back in the sheet.
Activate the workflow.
Add 2 more test rows. Verify auto-enrichment fires.
Save ENRICHMENT_V0.md: what it does, what API you used, what does not work yet, what you would need to productize this for a client.

tasks: Google Sheet set up with 5 real target companies
Workflow runs end to end: row in, enrichment out
Workflow active and firing on new rows
stretchGoals: Add a second enrichment source as fallback if first API fails
Add AI enrichment: use Claude or OpenAI to generate a one-line 'why target this company' note
Export the workflow as JSON and commit to GitHub as a portfolio piece
completionCriteria: Workflow solves a real problem you would charge money for
You understand every node, no mystery boxes

publicCommitment.template: Day 13 of 90. First real n8n build: automated lead enrichment. Drop a company name into a sheet, get domain / industry / size / LinkedIn back in 10 seconds. This is v0.1 of the SDR-in-a-Box system I will sell. Code on GitHub.

days.reflectionPrompt: Today I built something that a real buyer would pay for. If a client asked me to productize this, what would the next 5 things I would add? Those 5 things are my upgrade roadmap.
days.artifact: ENRICHMENT_V0.md + exported workflow JSON in GitHub

days.title: Week 1 retrospective and portfolio commit
days.weekMission: Ship your first 3 real n8n workflows
days.hook: End of Week 1. Six days of n8n behind you. You went from zero n8n knowledge to a working lead enrichment workflow. Today you consolidate: commit everything to GitHub, write a public recap, and honestly assess what still feels shaky. The recap post you write today will be the most credibility-building content you create all month.
days.objective: Consolidate Week 1 learnings into GitHub portfolio assets and a public build log post.
learningOutcome: Have 3 n8n workflows exported as JSON and committed to GitHub
Publish a Week 1 recap that positions you as a builder
Identify the 1 area from Week 1 that still feels weak

preWork.instructions: Review the week before writing about it.
prompts: Which day of Week 1 taught me the most?
Which day surprised me? (positive or negative)
If I had to redo one day, which and why?

steps: In n8n, export these 3 workflows as JSON: Daily Reminder (Day 10), Authenticated API call (Day 11), Lead Enrichment v0.1 (Day 13).
Create a new folder in your ammar-blueprint repo: /workflows/week-1/.
Drop the 3 JSON files in.
Create /workflows/week-1/README.md explaining what each workflow does and how to import into n8n.
Commit and push.
Write WEEK_1_RECAP.md in project root. Structure: what I built, what I learned, what still feels shaky, metrics (hours spent, workflows shipped, concepts understood), Week 2 preview.
Publish a LinkedIn post or X thread based on the recap. Hook: 'I knew zero about n8n 7 days ago. Today I have 3 working automations committed to GitHub. Here is what I built:'

stretchGoals: Record a 3-minute Loom walkthrough of the Lead Enrichment workflow and embed in LinkedIn post
DM 2 people from your Day 6 target list with the Loom as a free gift (NOT a pitch, just value)
completionCriteria: GitHub has visible /workflows/week-1/ folder with 3 JSONs
LinkedIn or X has a public Week 1 recap
You can explain each workflow in 60 seconds

publicCommitment.template: Week 1 of 12 complete. I learned n8n from zero and shipped 3 working automations. All code public on GitHub [link]. Next week: Make.com, so I can tell clients which tool fits their stack. Full journey at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app

days.reflectionPrompt: If I could tell Day 8 Ammar one thing about Week 1, what would it be? That thing is my first hard-earned belief about this craft.
days.artifact: /workflows/week-1/ folder on GitHub + WEEK_1_RECAP.md + public recap post

weeks.title: Make.com Foundations
weeks.summary: Learn Make.com and run a head-to-head with n8n. Rebuild Week 1's best workflow in Make to feel the difference. Pick your primary platform for the rest of the program. Knowing both makes you a consultant who can speak a client's existing stack, not push one tool.

days.title: What Make.com is and why clients often already use it
days.weekMission: Know both platforms, commit to one primary
days.hook: Most B2B SaaS companies that already automate something are using Make or Zapier, not n8n. If you only know n8n, you lose half your prospects in the first call. Today you meet Make properly. Not to switch. To understand the competitor your clients already paid for.
days.objective: Understand what Make.com is, how it differs from n8n, and when it is the right choice for a client.
learningOutcome: Explain Make.com in one sentence to a buyer
Name the 3 situations where Make beats n8n
Name the 3 situations where n8n beats Make

preWork.instructions: Predict before you learn.
prompts: What do I think the difference between Make and n8n is?
Which one do I think is easier for a non-technical founder?
Which one do I think is more powerful for AI work?

videos.title: Make.com vs n8n: The 2026 Definitive Comparison
videos.creator: NoCodeRank
keyTimestamps: Focus on 'who is it for' section
Skim pricing, read closely the AI capabilities section

videos.title: n8n vs Make (Integromat): Definitive Comparison for 2026
videos.creator: Cipher Projects
keyTimestamps: Focus on AI-native automation section
Note the cost math: Make counts per-module, n8n counts per-execution

steps: Write MAKE_DECISION.md in your project.
Section 1: Make.com in one sentence.
Section 2: 3 scenarios where Make wins over n8n.
Section 3: 3 scenarios where n8n wins over Make.
Section 4: your prediction - which platform will your ICP more often already use, and what does that mean for you as a consultant?

stretchGoals: Sign up for Make.com free tier (1,000 operations/month)
Scroll through Make's template library, note 3 templates you could sell as services
completionCriteria: Can explain Make in one sentence
Can articulate when to use which platform

publicCommitment.template: Day 15 of 90. Most automation freelancers only know one tool. That is a weakness when clients already run on the other. Learning Make.com this week to be a real consultant, not a one-trick builder.

days.reflectionPrompt: If a SaaS founder tells me 'we already have Zapier, can we just use that?' - how would I answer? What is my actual opinion, not a sales pitch?

days.title: Build your first Make scenario
days.weekMission: Know both platforms, commit to one primary
days.hook: Make is easier than n8n for beginners. That is not an insult to n8n, it is a feature of Make. Today you feel it. You build your first scenario and notice how the drag-and-drop makes certain things obvious that n8n hides behind JSON. This is why Make has more non-technical users.
days.objective: Build a working Make scenario end to end with trigger, action, and data flow.
learningOutcome: Navigate the Make scenario builder
Use modules, connect them, map fields
Execute and schedule scenarios
Read the Operations counter and understand pricing implications

preWork.instructions: Pick what you want to build today. Rebuild the Day 10 n8n workflow in Make for apples-to-apples comparison.
prompts: Recall Day 10: trigger on schedule, send a message. Same thing in Make.
What was hard in n8n Day 10 that I am curious to compare?

videos.title: Make.com tutorial for beginners 2026
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Pick a 2025 or 2026 video
Focus on scenario builder + first scenario

steps: Log into Make.com free tier.
Create a new scenario. Add Schedule trigger. Set to run every day at a specific time.
Add Gmail, Slack, or Telegram module. Same channel as Day 10 so you can compare.
Connect modules with the drag line. Map the message text.
Run once manually. Verify delivery.
Schedule and activate. Wait one cycle. Verify automatic fire.
Deactivate.
Open the Operations counter in your account. Note how many operations today's tests consumed.
Save FIRST_MAKE_SCENARIO.md: what built faster in Make than n8n, what built slower, and the 'feel' difference.

stretchGoals: Import a template from Make's library and study how it is built
Try Make's Maia AI assistant to build a scenario from a text prompt
completionCriteria: Working scenario in Make
Can compare the build experience to n8n Day 10

publicCommitment.template: Day 16 of 90. Built the same workflow in both n8n and Make. Here is what surprised me: [one thing].

days.reflectionPrompt: Make felt different from n8n in what specific way? Was that difference 'easier' or 'more limited'? The honest answer determines which tool I lean toward.

days.title: Make advanced: routers, filters, aggregators
days.weekMission: Know both platforms, commit to one primary
days.hook: Real scenarios branch. If lead size is enterprise, send to sales. If SMB, send to self-serve. Make's router, filter, and aggregator are what turn linear workflows into business logic. Today you learn them.
days.objective: Build a branching Make scenario using router, filter, and aggregator modules.
learningOutcome: Route data paths based on conditions
Filter out items that do not match criteria
Aggregate multiple items into one output

preWork.instructions: Imagine a realistic branching workflow.
prompts: A new lead comes in. ARR > $5M goes to sales. ARR < $5M goes to self-serve. How would I build that?
What should happen if ARR is missing from the data?

videos.title: Make.com routers and filters tutorial
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Focus on router behavior and filter conditions

steps: New scenario: 'Lead Router Test'.
Trigger: Manual (for testing).
Add a Set Variable module that fakes 3 sample leads (name, company, ARR).
Iterate over the array.
Add a Router module after the iterator.
Path 1: Filter ARR > 5000000, then send to Slack channel 'sales'.
Path 2: Filter ARR <= 5000000, then send to Slack channel 'self-serve'.
Path 3 (fallback): no ARR data, send to Slack channel 'needs-review'.
Run manually with sample data. Verify each path fires correctly.
Save MAKE_BRANCHING.md describing the pattern.

tasks: Each path fires based on filter conditions
stretchGoals: Add an Aggregator that collects all 'enterprise' leads from a batch run and sends one summary message
Rebuild this exact scenario in n8n (using IF node). Time both. Which was faster?
completionCriteria: Branching logic works in Make
You understand when routers vs filters apply

publicCommitment.template: Day 17 of 90. Most automation is not linear. Real workflows branch based on business logic. Learned Make routers today. This is where $500 scenarios become $2,500 scenarios.

days.reflectionPrompt: Linear workflows are easy. Branching is what clients pay for. What is the first branching workflow I would build for my ICP?

days.title: Rebuild Day 13 lead enrichment in Make
days.weekMission: Know both platforms, commit to one primary
days.hook: Same input, same output, different tool. Today you rebuild the Day 13 n8n lead enrichment in Make, module for module. By end of day you have a direct apples-to-apples comparison. You will feel one of them as 'home' and one as 'tolerable'. That is your primary platform pick.
days.objective: Rebuild Day 13's lead enrichment workflow in Make.com to produce a direct comparison.
learningOutcome: Replicate a multi-step workflow across platforms
Recognize which tool's mental model fits yours
Make an informed primary-platform decision

preWork.instructions: Set up the same Google Sheet you used Day 13, or duplicate.
prompts: Same sheet, same API, same output columns
What went wrong on Day 13 that I now know how to avoid?

videos.title: Make.com Google Sheets integration tutorial
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Focus on Google Sheets + HTTP modules

steps: New Make scenario: 'Lead Enrichment - Make version'.
Trigger: Google Sheets new row.
HTTP module: same enrichment API as Day 13.
Data mapping: same output fields.
Google Sheets module: update row.
Add error handling.
Test with the same 5 companies.
Save ENRICHMENT_V0_MAKE.md.
Update ENRICHMENT_V0.md from Day 13 with a comparison section: time to build in each, gotchas in each, which one I would pick to productize.

tasks: Make scenario produces same enrichment output as n8n version
Both versions tested with identical inputs
stretchGoals: Time how long each platform took you to build the same workflow. Total including debug.
Calculate cost at 100 enrichments/month on each platform (n8n self-host vs Make free vs Make Core)
completionCriteria: Two working enrichment workflows, one per platform
Direct comparison written

publicCommitment.template: Day 18 of 90. Built the same lead enrichment in n8n and Make. Results: [one-sentence comparison]. This is the level of due diligence your consultant should do before recommending a stack.

days.reflectionPrompt: Which tool felt like home and which felt like work? That visceral reaction matters more than any feature list.
days.artifact: 2 working enrichment flows + comparison section in ENRICHMENT_V0.md

days.title: Pick your primary: make the decision
days.weekMission: Know both platforms, commit to one primary
days.hook: You have real data now. Today you commit. This decision is not forever but it sets the 70 remaining days. Most consultants who try to stay platform-neutral ship worse work than those who pick a side and get deep. You are picking the one you will be excellent with.
days.objective: Commit to n8n or Make as your primary platform for Weeks 3-12. Document the decision with reasoning.
learningOutcome: Make a platform commitment based on real experience, not hype
Know how to still consult on the OTHER platform when a client needs it
Set up your positioning to reflect the choice

preWork.instructions: Score both platforms on dimensions that matter.
prompts: Build speed for YOU: n8n or Make, which felt faster by Day 18?
Your ICP (Western B2B SaaS $2-20M ARR): which platform would they trust more?
AI agent work: which platform handles agents natively in 2026?
Cost at scale: which is cheaper at 5-10 clients?
Portfolio signal: which shows more builder depth on GitHub?

steps: Write PRIMARY_PLATFORM.md in your project.
Section 1: the pick (n8n or Make, one word).
Section 2: 5 reasons for the pick, each tied to your specific situation.
Section 3: when I would still recommend the OTHER platform to a client.
Section 4: what this means for my positioning. Update OFFER.md if needed.
Update ammar-blueprint README and LinkedIn headline if your platform pick changes your claimed expertise.
Commit all changes.

tasks: OFFER.md reviewed and updated if needed
README and LinkedIn adjusted if primary platform changed
stretchGoals: Write a LinkedIn post: 'I tested n8n and Make for 11 days. Here is what I picked and why.' This is credibility-building content.
completionCriteria: Decision made, documented, and reflected in public positioning

publicCommitment.template: Day 19 of 90. Decision made: [n8n/Make] is my primary automation platform for Weeks 3-12. Here are the 3 biggest reasons: [your reasons]. If you are a SaaS founder deciding between them, DM me - happy to share what I learned.

days.reflectionPrompt: Making this decision required data I did not have 11 days ago. What other decisions in my life would I benefit from waiting on until I have real data?
days.artifact: PRIMARY_PLATFORM.md + updated OFFER.md + public decision post

days.title: Advanced features in your chosen platform
days.weekMission: Know both platforms, commit to one primary
days.hook: You picked your platform yesterday. Today you go deeper on it. One day of advanced features to lift you from 'I can build basic workflows' to 'I can solve specific client problems'. Pick 3 features from the advanced list and build mini-demos.
days.objective: Learn 3 advanced features of your primary platform by building mini-demos of each.
learningOutcome: Use 3 advanced features you had not touched before
Know which advanced features translate to client value
Have 3 more small workflows in your portfolio

preWork.instructions: Pick your 3 features.
prompts: For n8n: expressions, code node, webhook trigger, sub-workflows, variables, data storage
For Make: data stores, aggregators, iterators, JSON modules, text parser, webhook responses
Pick 3 from your platform's list, ones that would make a client say 'oh that is cool'.

videos.title: Advanced features for your picked platform
videos.creator: Search YouTube for each feature you picked
keyTimestamps: Watch one 15-min video per feature
Do not watch all 3 then build. Watch 1, build 1, repeat.

steps: Feature 1: watch video, build mini-demo, save workflow.
Feature 2: same.
Feature 3: same.
Save ADVANCED_FEATURES.md: what each feature does, one client problem each could solve, one gotcha you hit.

tasks: 3 new workflows saved in your platform account
stretchGoals: Export all 3 mini-demos to /workflows/week-2/ on GitHub
Turn one of them into a short tutorial thread on X or LinkedIn
completionCriteria: 3 advanced features understood by building, not just watching
Each maps to a concrete client use case

publicCommitment.template: Day 20 of 90. Advanced [platform] features shipped. Most freelancers stop at basics. The gap between 'can build' and 'can sell' is these features.

days.reflectionPrompt: Which of the 3 features clicked instantly and which took the most re-reading? The one that took longest is probably your biggest growth area.

days.title: Week 2 retrospective and platform commitment post
days.weekMission: Know both platforms, commit to one primary
days.hook: Week 2 done. You now have fluency in one platform and working knowledge of the other. That puts you ahead of 80% of AI automation freelancers who only know one tool. Today you consolidate and publish.
days.objective: Commit Week 2 artifacts to GitHub and publish a Week 2 retrospective.
learningOutcome: Have Week 2 workflows exported and committed
Publish a retrospective that signals consultant-grade thinking
Plan Week 3 with clearer context

preWork.instructions: Review before writing.
prompts: What was the biggest unlock of Week 2?
What do I still feel shaky on?
If I were hiring me as a consultant now, what would I trust me to build?

steps: Export Week 2 workflows to /workflows/week-2/ folder on GitHub.
Update root README to show Week 2 complete.
Write WEEK_2_RECAP.md.
Publish public retrospective on LinkedIn or X.
Preview Week 3: AI agents. Look ahead at Day 22-28 briefly so Monday does not start cold.

stretchGoals: Update your Upwork profile skills to include both n8n and Make
Send the recap post to 3 people from your Day 6 target list via DM
completionCriteria: GitHub shows Week 1 and Week 2 folders
Public recap live
You feel ready for Week 3

publicCommitment.template: Week 2 of 12 complete. 11 days into n8n + Make. Picked [platform] as my primary for AI automation work. Why: [your reason]. Next week: AI agents. Track at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app

days.reflectionPrompt: Two weeks in, am I on pace? If not, where am I slipping - effort, energy, or conviction? Each has a different fix.

weeks.title: AI Agents
weeks.summary: Where workflows become intelligent. Today's workflows execute rules. Agents make decisions. Learn the AI Agent node in your primary platform, prompt engineering for workflows, memory, and tool-calling. By end of week you have a working agent that does real work a client would pay for.

days.title: Workflows vs agents: when to use which
days.weekMission: Ship your first AI agent that does real client work
days.hook: Every AI automation pitch in 2026 says the word 'agent'. Most of those pitches describe things that are actually just workflows with a Claude API call bolted on. Today you learn the real difference. This is your vocabulary for client calls.
days.objective: Distinguish between workflows and agents and know when each is the right choice.
learningOutcome: Define an AI agent in one sentence to a non-technical buyer
Name 3 tasks that are agent-appropriate and 3 that are workflow-appropriate
Recognize agent-washing in other people's pitches

preWork.instructions: Test your current mental model.
prompts: What do I think the difference between a workflow and an agent is?
If a client asks 'can you build me an agent?' what do I say?
Give one example of a task an agent handles better than a workflow.

videos.title: Agents vs Workflows: Pick the Right Tool with Nick Saraev
videos.creator: n8n Masterclass podcast
keyTimestamps: Focus on Nick's distinction between the two
Listen for his examples of when agents are wrong

videos.title: How to Build AI Agents with n8n: Complete 2026 Guide
videos.creator: Strapi blog
keyTimestamps: Read the Memory and Tool-Calling sections

steps: Write AGENTS_VS_WORKFLOWS.md.
Section 1: one-sentence definition of each.
Section 2: 3 tasks agents excel at.
Section 3: 3 tasks workflows are better for (hint: predictable, deterministic, high-volume).
Section 4: Nick's rule of thumb for picking.
Section 5: your ICP's typical problems - which ones are agent-shaped?

stretchGoals: List 5 'agents' you have seen promoted online. Reclassify each as workflow vs true agent vs unclear.
Draft a LinkedIn post called 'Agent-washing is the new AI-washing'
completionCriteria: Can articulate the difference verbally in 30 seconds
Can call out pitches that misuse 'agent' language

publicCommitment.template: Day 22 of 90. Most 'AI agents' being sold in 2026 are just workflows with a prompt. Real agents make decisions. Here is the test: [one-sentence test you learned].

days.reflectionPrompt: If my ICP asked me to build an 'agent', my first job is to figure out whether they actually need one. What 3 questions would I ask them to find out?

days.title: Your first AI node: Claude API in your workflow
days.weekMission: Ship your first AI agent that does real client work
days.hook: Before agents, you need the atomic building block: calling an LLM from inside a workflow. Today you wire Claude API into n8n or Make. The output of this single call is raw material your entire AI automation practice builds on.
days.objective: Make authenticated Claude API calls from your primary platform and use the response downstream.
learningOutcome: Set up Anthropic API credentials in your platform
Send a prompt to Claude and receive structured output
Use prompt engineering to shape the response
Parse JSON output and use downstream

preWork.instructions: Get your credentials ready.
prompts: Sign up at console.anthropic.com. Create a test API key. Save it to a secure place.
Put $5-10 in credits. This gets you hundreds of test calls.
Pick what you want Claude to do today. Suggest: input a company name, output a one-line outreach hook.

videos.title: Anthropic API + n8n tutorial
videos.creator: Search YouTube for your platform
keyTimestamps: Focus on credential setup and first call

steps: In your platform, add Anthropic API credentials using your key.
New workflow: 'Claude First Call'.
Trigger: Manual.
Set module: create a variable 'companyName' with value 'Linear'.
Anthropic node: send a prompt like 'Write a 15-word outreach hook for a B2B SaaS company called {companyName}. Focus on a specific pain your audience feels. No greetings.'
Execute. Verify Claude returns a hook.
Iterate: tweak the prompt 3 times. Note how output changes.
Save an output you like as a reference.
Now swap the model: try Haiku (cheaper/faster) vs Sonnet (smarter). Note the difference.
Save FIRST_AI_CALL.md with: the prompt that worked, the output, and the cost per call.

tasks: Anthropic credentials set up in platform
stretchGoals: Run the same prompt through OpenAI API for comparison
Track cost per call in your FIRST_AI_CALL.md
completionCriteria: Claude API is wired into your primary platform
You have at least one useful output saved

publicCommitment.template: Day 23 of 90. First Claude API call inside n8n. One workflow can now generate personalized outreach hooks at scale. This is the atomic unit of modern AI automation.

days.reflectionPrompt: The output quality of Claude is directly proportional to the specificity of my prompt. What prompt skills would I need to level up for this to become a repeatable client offer?

days.title: Prompt engineering for workflows
days.weekMission: Ship your first AI agent that does real client work
days.hook: Yesterday Claude generated output. Today you make that output reliable. In production, a 90% accurate prompt is a problem. A 99.5% accurate prompt is a product. Today you learn to turn guesswork into deterministic output.
days.objective: Write prompts that produce structured, reliable output suitable for production workflows.
learningOutcome: Structure prompts with role, context, task, output format
Use JSON mode or structured output constraints
Test prompts across diverse inputs for reliability
Know when to fall back to deterministic logic

preWork.instructions: Your Day 23 prompt is probably decent but not production-ready. Diagnose it.
prompts: Run my Day 23 prompt with 10 different company names. Does it return consistent format every time?
What happens when a company name is weird (emoji, non-English, acronyms)?

videos.title: Anthropic prompt engineering docs
videos.creator: Anthropic
keyTimestamps: Read: Be clear and direct, Use examples (multishot), Use XML tags, Chain prompts

steps: Rewrite Day 23's prompt using Anthropic's best practices.
Add a role: 'You are a B2B SaaS outbound specialist.'
Add context: '{companyName} is a B2B SaaS company. Your audience is the VP Sales.'
Add task: 'Write a 15-word outreach hook that names a specific pain.'
Add output format: 'Return ONLY the hook. No preamble, no quotes, no explanation.'
Add 2 few-shot examples of good output.
Test with 10 company names. Measure: output length, format consistency, quality.
Iterate 3 times. Track what improved and what regressed.
Save PROMPT_V2.md: final prompt, test results, lessons.

tasks: Prompt rewritten using Anthropic best practices
stretchGoals: Force structured JSON output. Parse and use downstream.
Add a 'verify' step: second Claude call to grade the first call's output
completionCriteria: Prompt produces 9+/10 usable outputs
Output format is consistent enough to use downstream

publicCommitment.template: Day 24 of 90. A prompt that works 9/10 times is a demo. A prompt that works 99/100 is a product. Here is the structure that moved me from demo to production.

days.reflectionPrompt: The gap between a prompt that 'works' and a prompt I would trust in production is bigger than I thought. What does this tell me about how much prompt engineering skill my offer requires?

days.title: AI Agent node: your first real agent
days.weekMission: Ship your first AI agent that does real client work
days.hook: Today is the big one. An agent is not a workflow that calls an LLM. It is an LLM that chooses which workflows to call. Today you build one. Small scope. Real capability. By end of day your agent can reason about a task, pick the right tool, and execute.
days.objective: Build a working AI agent using n8n's AI Agent node (or Make equivalent) that can choose between multiple tools.
learningOutcome: Configure an AI Agent node with memory and tools
Give the agent 2-3 tools it can choose between
Understand the decision loop: reason, act, observe, repeat

preWork.instructions: Design before you build.
prompts: My agent's one job today: one sentence. Suggest: research a company and decide whether it fits our ICP.
What are the 2-3 tools my agent needs? (web search, company enrichment, calculator)
How will I know the agent worked? (success criterion)

videos.title: n8n AI Agent node complete tutorial 2026
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Focus on tool-calling and memory

videos.title: Cole Medin n8n AI agent masterclass
videos.creator: Cole Medin
keyTimestamps: Deeper technical depth than Nick on agents specifically

steps: New workflow: 'ICP Fit Agent'.
Trigger: Manual with input field 'companyName'.
Add AI Agent node. Use Claude Sonnet as model.
Add Memory sub-node: Window Buffer Memory.
Add Tool 1: HTTP Request to Apollo or Clearbit API (company enrichment).
Add Tool 2: HTTP Request to a simple web search API (Tavily free tier).

Agent system prompt: 'You are an ICP fit analyst. Given a company name, decide if it is B2B SaaS, $2-20M ARR, founder-led GTM. Use tools to gather data. Return a fit score 0-100 and 2-sentence reasoning. Return as JSON.'
Execute with 'Linear'. Watch the agent reason, call tools, synthesize.
Test with 5 diverse companies: one strong fit, one weak fit, one edge case.
Save AGENT_V1.md: agent's correct calls, mistakes, and how you would tune the system prompt to fix mistakes.

tasks: AI Agent node wired with model + memory + 2 tools
stretchGoals: Add a 3rd tool: LinkedIn scraping via Apify
Add structured output parser so downstream nodes can use the fit score directly
Add error handling for when tools fail
completionCriteria: Agent makes decisions, not just text generation
You understand how it chose tools

publicCommitment.template: Day 25 of 90. Shipped my first real AI agent. Input: company name. Output: ICP fit score with reasoning. This is the building block for SDR-in-a-Box and the entire Weeks 4-6 portfolio. Code on GitHub.

days.reflectionPrompt: An agent is slower and more expensive than a workflow. When is that tradeoff worth it? What pattern in my observations tells me my agent was the right tool, not an over-engineered workflow?
days.artifact: AGENT_V1.md + working ICP Fit Agent workflow

days.title: Memory and context: agents that remember
days.weekMission: Ship your first AI agent that does real client work
days.hook: A stateless agent is a chatbot with amnesia. The real power comes from memory: remembering the last 10 interactions, recalling previous decisions, learning from past runs. Today you add memory to your agent and feel the difference.
days.objective: Add persistent memory to your AI agent so it can carry context across runs.
learningOutcome: Configure Window Buffer Memory vs persistent memory stores
Know when to use memory vs when to keep agents stateless
Implement a simple RAG pattern for agent knowledge

preWork.instructions: Think about what memory unlocks.
prompts: What would my ICP Fit Agent from Day 25 do better if it remembered previous analyses?
What is a realistic piece of 'knowledge' I want my agent to have persistently?

videos.title: n8n memory + RAG for AI agents
videos.creator: Search YouTube
keyTimestamps: Focus on Buffer Memory vs Vector Store Memory

steps: Extend Day 25's ICP Fit Agent.
Replace default memory with Window Buffer Memory (20 turns).
Test: run the agent on 3 companies in a row. Ask it on run 3 to compare these 3 companies you just analyzed. Verify it remembers.
Advanced: add a simple vector store (Pinecone or Supabase free tier) with 10 sample ICP descriptions.
Agent system prompt update: Before deciding fit, search the ICP knowledge base for similar past analyses.
Test: does the agent's reasoning improve when it has RAG context?
Save MEMORY_AND_RAG.md: what changed, what improved, what broke.

tasks: Buffer memory working across 3 sequential runs
Stretch: simple RAG with vector store set up
stretchGoals: Populate the vector store with 50 real ICP examples from your Week 0 work
Test whether RAG-enhanced agent is more accurate than baseline agent
completionCriteria: Agent passes the 'remember this conversation' test
You understand the tradeoffs of memory vs stateless

publicCommitment.template: Day 26 of 90. Added memory to my ICP agent. It now remembers past analyses and compares against them. This is the difference between 'one-shot tool' and 'ongoing team member'.

days.reflectionPrompt: Memory adds cost, complexity, and potential for the agent to hallucinate. When is memory worth it for a client? When is it over-engineering?
days.artifact: MEMORY_AND_RAG.md + upgraded ICP Fit Agent with memory

days.title: Agent guardrails and evaluation
days.weekMission: Ship your first AI agent that does real client work
days.hook: A brilliant agent that occasionally hallucinates wrong data is a liability. In production, you need evaluation (does it work reliably?) and guardrails (what happens when it does not?). Clients will not pay for agents they cannot trust.
days.objective: Add evaluation and guardrails to your agent to make it production-viable.
learningOutcome: Design test cases for agent reliability
Implement guardrails: content filters, fallback paths, human-in-the-loop
Know when an agent is production-ready vs demo-only

preWork.instructions: Write a test plan before you test.
prompts: What does 'wrong' look like for my ICP Fit Agent?
What are 5 edge cases that would break it? (weird company names, tiny companies, B2C)
If it goes wrong, who notices and when?

videos.title: AI agent evaluation and guardrails
videos.creator: Search YouTube or read Anthropic posts
keyTimestamps: Focus on human-in-the-loop patterns and confidence scoring

steps: Write AGENT_TESTS.md with 10 test cases: 5 should pass, 5 should fail (or produce low confidence).
Run agent against all 10 cases. Score actual outputs.
Identify failure modes.
Add guardrails: low-confidence threshold routes to 'needs human review' path, output validation checks score is 0-100, fallback to simpler rule-based logic if agent errors.
Re-run all 10 tests. Verify improved reliability.
Save AGENT_EVALUATION.md: test results, guardrails added, remaining known weaknesses.

tasks: Guardrails added for at least 2 failure modes
stretchGoals: Set up a scheduled test run: every day, agent runs all 10 test cases, reports pass rate to you
Write a client-facing explanation of your agent's reliability: works X% of the time, with these known limits
completionCriteria: Agent has written test cases and actual pass/fail scores
At least 2 guardrails prevent known failure modes
You could explain its reliability to a client honestly

publicCommitment.template: Day 27 of 90. Added evaluation and guardrails to my agent. Most AI automation agencies skip this. It is what separates 'cool demo at a conference' from 'system a client trusts in production'.

days.reflectionPrompt: Before today my agent felt like magic. Now it feels like engineering. Which framing will my clients value more?

days.title: Week 3 retrospective and agent portfolio piece
days.weekMission: Ship your first AI agent that does real client work
days.hook: End of Technical Foundation phase. You have gone from zero automation knowledge to a production-ready AI agent in 21 days. Today you consolidate and write the most portfolio-shaping content of the program so far.
days.objective: Ship Week 3 to GitHub and publish a substantial recap that positions you as an agent builder, not just a workflow tinkerer.
learningOutcome: Have complete AI agent workflow on GitHub with README
Published a content piece that demonstrates expertise
Ready to move into portfolio-system building (Weeks 4-6)

preWork.instructions: Think big picture before recapping.
prompts: If a client saw my Week 3 work, would they hire me as an agent builder? Why or why not?
What is still missing that I need to build in Week 4?

steps: Export ICP Fit Agent workflow JSON to /workflows/week-3/.
Write /workflows/week-3/README.md: what the agent does, how to import, API keys needed, test cases.
Write WEEK_3_RECAP.md.
Phase 1 complete milestone post: 21 days in. Technical foundation done. Here is what I built: [list]. Next: 3 portfolio systems.
Include a Loom walkthrough of the agent in action (3-5 min).
Post on LinkedIn with Loom embedded.

stretchGoals: Send the Loom directly to 3 people from Day 6 target list
Update Upwork profile with agent-building skills
Update OFFER.md tier 3 retainer description now that you can actually build agents
completionCriteria: GitHub visibly shows 3 weeks of work
LinkedIn has a substantial post closing out Phase 1
You feel confident moving into portfolio systems

publicCommitment.template: Phase 1 of 3 complete. 21 days. 3 weeks. n8n + Make + AI agents. All workflows on GitHub. Loom walkthrough of my first agent in this post. Phase 2 starts Monday: building 3 portfolio-ready systems I will sell as products. Live journey at ammar-blueprint.vercel.app

days.reflectionPrompt: 21 days ago I could not build a workflow. Today I have a working AI agent. What does this tell me about what else is possible in the next 21 days?
days.artifact: /workflows/week-3/ on GitHub + WEEK_3_RECAP.md + Loom walkthrough + phase 1 post

weeks.title: Portfolio System #1: SDR-in-a-Box
weeks.summary: Coming soon. Batch 2 of the curriculum.
